lead: Continuous Integration with Jenkins + Claude Code
contents: The Problem: “I’ll Integrate Later” What Is Jenkins? What Is Nexus? The Jenkinsfile: Pipeline as Code Jenkins in Action “The Build Is Red, I’ll Fix It Later” Jenkins Pipeline as Trace with Spans SLOs: Measuring Pipeline Health AI-Augmented: Claude Code Creates and Debugs Pipelines CI Pipeline = Everything You Learned Exercise 5.1: Write a Jenkinsfile Exercise 5.2: Break the Pipeline Exercise 5.3: Measure Your Pipeline Health GitHub Actions: The Modern Alternative AI Inside the Pipeline Claude Code in CI Pipelines Session Isolation: Writer ≠ Reviewer Exercise 5.4: AI-Powered Review in Print Mode Module 5: Key Takeaways

items: Developer A works for 2 weeks on Feature X
Developer B works for 2 weeks on Feature Y
They merge on Friday afternoon
Result: 200 conflicts. Build broken. Tests fail. Nobody goes home.

body.quote: CI = merge and verify multiple times per day.

items: An automation server for CI/CD pipelines
Open source, industry standard since 2011
Watches your Git repository for changes
Automatically: builds, tests, reports, deploys

body.code: Git push → Jenkins detects → Build → Test → Quality → Report | Green: ready to merge Red: fix your code

title: What Is Nexus?

body.code: You run: mvn deploy Your .jar file | v Nexus Repository (artifact storage) | v Other teams download your .jar CI pipelines fetch dependencies Production servers pull release builds Think of it as “npm registry” for Java.
body.note: Git stores source code. Nexus stores compiled artifacts. Never mix them.

table.note: This is why target/ is in .gitignore — Nexus handles built artifacts.

desc: Where do your built artifacts go? Not Git — that’s for source code only.

body.code: pipeline { agent any stages { stage('Build') { steps { sh 'mvn clean compile' } } stage('Test') { steps { sh 'mvn test' } } stage('Quality') { steps { sh 'mvn checkstyle:check pmd:check spotbugs:check' } } stage('Package') { steps { sh 'mvn package' } } } }
body.note: This file lives in Git alongside your code. Pipeline is versioned just like code.

body.code: git push origin main | v Jenkins webhook trigger — detects new commit | v Stage 1: Build mvn compile [32s] | v Stage 2: Test mvn test [1m 15s] | v Stage 3: Quality checkstyle + pmd [28s] | v Stage 4: Package mvn package [12s] | v Result: GREEN (all stages passed) or RED (failed stage stops pipeline)
body.note: A failed stage stops everything. Stage 3 quality violation? Package never runs. That’s the point.

body.card: What you see in the Jenkins dashboard:
items: Green row = all stages passed, artifact published
Red row = a stage failed, shows which one and why
Blue ball = build is running right now
Build history = every push, every result, timestamped

title: “The Build Is Red, I’ll Fix It Later”

body.card: A red build blocks the entire team:
items: Nobody can merge while the build is broken
The person who broke it must fix it immediately
“Fix it later” means it gets worse

body.quote: Professional rule: A broken build is the #1 priority.

title: Jenkins Pipeline as Trace with Spans
desc: Your Jenkins pipeline follows the same observability pattern as AI agent traces.

table.note: Same structure. A trace is a sequence of spans (steps) with status, duration, and outcome.

title: SLOs: Measuring Pipeline Health
desc: Measuring system health over time, not just right now.

items: Service Level Objective — a measurable target for how well something should work
Not a one-time check — tracked over time (e.g. “97% of builds pass per month”)
When an SLO is violated, the team must act — fix the pipeline before adding features

rows: Passed builds / total builds per week
Average time from commit to result
Below 70% — rules too strict or code too sloppy
Tests that pass/fail randomly without code changes
How long a broken build stays red
Above 3 days — broken is the new normal
table.note: You can track these in Jenkins with build history. No special tools needed — just pay attention.

body.quote: If your pipeline is red 30% of the time, nobody trusts it. If nobody trusts it, nobody runs it. Then you have no CI.

title: AI-Augmented: Claude Code Creates & Debugs Pipelines

body.code: You: Create a Jenkinsfile for my Calculator project with build, test, quality, and package stages.
You: My Jenkins pipeline fails at the Test stage. Here’s the error: [paste log]

body.quote: Claude Code reads verbose Jenkins logs and extracts the actual error.

body.code: Git push (Module 1) | v Maven compile (Module 2) — capability contract | v CheckStyle + PMD + SpotBugs (3) — regression gates | v JUnit + Mockito tests (4) — eval dataset | v Package + Deploy (Module 5) — traced execution

body.quote: Everything connects. CI automates the entire pipeline you built module by module.

title: cognitive-core: Evolutionary CI/CD Pipeline
desc: Different pipeline stages need different confidence levels.

body.code: # cognitive-core.conf — graduated fitness gates CC_FITNESS_LINT=60 # Local iteration (low bar) CC_FITNESS_COMMIT=80 # Reviewable code CC_FITNESS_TEST=85 # Integration ready CC_FITNESS_MERGE=90 # Near-production CC_FITNESS_DEPLOY=95 # Production-ready Why not 100% everywhere? Because a work-in-progress commit at 70% fitness is fine on a feature branch. But deploying it to production would be irresponsible.
body.note: No other CI tool does this. SonarQube has one threshold. Jenkins has pass/fail. cognitive-core has 5 graduated gates that match confidence to risk.

rows: Is this safe for the team?

items: Ask Claude Code to generate a Jenkinsfile for your project
Read every line and explain each stage
Add a stage that runs mvn dependency:tree and saves output
What happens if the Test stage fails — do later stages run?
Introduce a failing test
Run mvn test locally — confirm failure
Which Jenkinsfile stage catches this?
Fix the test
Introduce a CheckStyle violation — which stage catches it?

body.quote: Discussion: Why is stage order important?

body.card: Imagine your pipeline ran 100 times this month:
items: 92 succeeded, 5 failed on tests, 3 failed on quality
Average duration: 4 minutes
Longest run: 12 minutes
What is your success rate SLO? Is 92% acceptable?
What is your latency SLO? Is 4 minutes acceptable?
What should you investigate first: the test failures or quality failures?
Write a one-paragraph “pipeline health report” for your team lead

title: GitHub Actions: The Modern Alternative
desc: Jenkins is what we teach. GitHub Actions is what you will likely use at work. Same concepts, simpler setup.

body.code: # .github/workflows/ci.yml name: Java CI on: [push, pull_request] jobs: build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-java@v4 with: java-version: "21" - run: mvn clean verify

table.note: We teach Jenkins because understanding CI concepts matters more than memorizing one tool’s YAML syntax. The concepts are identical.

title: AI Inside the Pipeline
desc: The next step: AI as an automated participant in your CI/CD.

items: PR Review Bot: AI reviews every pull request automatically
Test Gap Detection: AI identifies untested code paths
Dependency Security: AI flags vulnerable libraries
Commit Validation: AI verifies messages are meaningful
Documentation Check: AI flags public APIs without Javadoc

body.quote: This is where AI moves from “developer assistant” to “pipeline participant” — automated, consistent, and always running.

title: Claude Code in CI Pipelines
desc: AI as an automated pipeline participant — non-interactive mode.

body.code: # Non-interactive mode for CI/CD claude -p "Review this project for quality issues" # Structured JSON output for machine parsing claude -p "List all PMD violations" \ --output-format json # With JSON schema for guaranteed format claude -p "Review for grading criteria" \ --output-format json \ --json-schema review-schema.json
body.note: The -p (print) flag runs Claude Code without interaction — perfect for CI jobs.

title: Session Isolation: Writer ≠ Reviewer
desc: A critical principle for AI in production pipelines.

body.code: Developer writes code with Claude Code (Session A) | v git push → CI pipeline triggers | v Claude Code -p reviews the PR (Session B) | v Separate AI instance = second pair of eyes
body.note: The Claude instance that wrote the code should NOT review it. Self-review is weak.

body.quote: This is exactly how human code review works: the author never approves their own PR.

title: Exercise 5.4: AI-Powered Review in Print Mode

items: Run: claude -p "Review my project against these 10 grading criteria" --output-format json
Examine the JSON output — is it structured? Parseable?
Add CLAUDE.md context and run again — does the review improve?
Discuss: How would you integrate this into a Jenkinsfile stage?

body.quote: This is how production teams add AI review gates to their pipelines.

items: CI = merge and verify frequently, not “integrate on Friday”
Jenkins automates: build, test, quality, package
Jenkinsfile = pipeline as code, versioned in Git
A red build = #1 priority to fix
CI connects all previous modules into one automated pipeline
Industry: Jenkins pipeline = trace with spans; pipeline health = SLOs
Claude Code helps: create Jenkinsfiles, debug logs, explain failures
Pipeline is only as good as your tests and quality gates
